title: Launch Google+ as a Facebook-class social network
body: Google's identity team is preparing to launch Google+ as a unified social layer competing with Facebook for primary social-network usage. The plan is to seed adoption by integrating Google+ identity across Search, Gmail, YouTube, Android, and Maps so existing Google users land on Google+ as a default identity surface.

assumptions.text: Forcing Google+ identity across Gmail / YouTube / Search will pull existing Google users into active social use of Google+ at sufficient rate to bootstrap network effects against Facebook.
assumptions.outcome_evidence: Google sunset Google+ for consumers in 2019 after years of declining engagement. Users created accounts under cross-product pressure but did not transition primary social activity from Facebook; Google+ developed a 'ghost town' reputation. The cross-product-pull mechanism turned forced sign-ups into dormant profiles, not active social use.

outcome.text: Consumer Google+ shut down 2019 after a security disclosure accelerated an already-planned wind-down. Engagement metrics never crossed Facebook competitive thresholds.

title: Launch Quibi as a mobile-first premium short-form video service
body: Quibi is preparing a 2020 launch as a subscription streaming service with TV-budget productions cut into 5-10 minute episodes designed exclusively for mobile viewing. The product bet is that on-the-go mobile viewers want premium short-form content and will pay subscription pricing for it.

assumptions.text: Premium short-form video viewing happens primarily on mobile during in-between moments (commute, queues, breaks) and a mobile-only product fits that demand.
assumptions.outcome_evidence: Quibi shut down in October 2020, ~6 months after its April launch, citing the COVID-19 pandemic eliminating the in-between-moments use case (commute, gym, queues). The mobile-only constraint compounded the problem — without TV / web fallback, viewers couldn't sub on existing-screen habits at home. The 'mobile-only premium short-form' demand turned out to be substantially smaller than the addressable market modelled at launch.

outcome.text: Quibi shut down October 2020 after failing to hit subscription targets; remaining content licensed to Roku.

title: Ship Microsoft Bob as a friendly metaphor-based novice OS shell
body: Microsoft is preparing to ship Bob — a Windows 3.1 shell replacement built around a residential-house metaphor (rooms, drawers, helpers) intended to lower the learning curve for first-time computer users. The bet is that a relatable spatial metaphor will outperform the abstract Program Manager / File Manager UI for novices.

assumptions.text: A residential-house metaphor maps cleanly enough onto computer functions (rooms = applications, drawers = folders) that novice users will perform tasks faster than they would with the existing Program Manager UI.
assumptions.outcome_evidence: Microsoft Bob shipped in 1995 to poor reviews and weak sales, was discontinued within ~12 months, and is widely cited as a UX cautionary tale. Reviewers noted that the metaphor introduced more cognitive load (mapping rooms to functions) than the abstract UI it replaced; novice users were not the underserved segment Bob assumed; experienced users found the patronizing tone actively painful. The metaphor-helps assumption did not translate from premise to measured user productivity.

outcome.text: Microsoft Bob discontinued ~1996 after weak sales and uniformly negative reviews. Several team members migrated to Bob-derived projects (Office Assistant / Clippy, also widely panned).

title: Launch the Amazon Fire Phone
body: Amazon is preparing the 2014 launch of the Fire Phone, an iOS/Android-class smartphone running Fire OS (Android-fork) and tightly integrated with Amazon Prime / shopping. The product bet is that Amazon's commerce ecosystem advantage plus differentiating hardware features (Dynamic Perspective 3D display, Firefly object recognition) will pull Prime users to switch from iOS/Android to Fire as a primary phone.

assumptions.text: Tight Amazon Prime / shopping integration plus a Prime-bundled subsidy is enough to pull existing Prime users to switch from iOS or Android to Fire OS as a primary phone.
assumptions.outcome_evidence: Amazon wrote down ~$170M of Fire Phone inventory in Q3 2014, ~3 months after launch, then discontinued the line. Most users' switching cost was dominated by app-ecosystem lock-in (iMessage, app library, Google services) rather than commerce integration. Prime convenience did not move switching at the price points offered. The hardware was abandoned after ~12 months.

outcome.text: Fire Phone discontinued late 2015 after ~$170M write-down. The team was reorganized; some technology folded into Echo line.

title: Replace the original Coca-Cola formula with New Coke
body: Coca-Cola is preparing the 1985 reformulation replacing the original Coca-Cola formula with a sweeter recipe (later branded New Coke). The decision is grounded in extensive blind-taste-test data showing the new formula outperforms both the original and Pepsi. The bet is that taste-preference data translates to purchasing behavior at scale.

assumptions.text: Blind-taste-test preference for the sweeter formula translates to actual purchasing behavior — consumers who prefer the taste in a blind comparison will buy and continue buying the new formula in the open market.
assumptions.outcome_evidence: Coca-Cola reintroduced the original formula as 'Coca-Cola Classic' within ~3 months of the New Coke launch under intense consumer protest. The taste-preference assumption ignored the brand-identity / nostalgia component of consumer attachment — for a heritage product, removing the original formula triggered backlash dominated by emotional / identity-based purchasing rather than taste-rational purchasing. Blind-test methodology measured the wrong axis.

outcome.text: Coca-Cola Classic reinstated July 1985 after consumer revolt. New Coke continued in parallel as a side product, eventually discontinued. The episode is widely studied as the canonical example of misreading consumer preference signals.
